question: To what extent do relevant laws, regulations, policies, and guidance provide a comprehensive framework for protection of personal data?
To what extent do relevant laws, regulations, policies, and guidance provide a comprehensive framework for protection of personal data?
response:
Togo has a comprehensive data protection Act voted in 2019. However, the country has not yet established the Agency that will enforce the law. 

(1) The Togolese Constitution of October 14, 1992, https://www.constituteproject.org/constitution/Togo_2007.pdf?lang=en 
(2) The Electronic Communications Act No. 2012-018  http://www.artp.tg/Download/Telecommunication/Loi/loi_2013-003_portant_modification_loi_2012-018_du_17_decembre_2012_communications_electorniques.pdf 
(3) The he Electronic Transactions Act No. 2017-007 adopted in 2017, http://jo.gouv.tg/sites/default/files/JO/JOS_07_07_2017-62E%20ANNEE%20N%C2%B021%20QUARTO.pdf
(4) The Data Protection Act (DPA) https://numerique.gouv.tg/wp-content/uploads/2020/01/Loi-n-2019-014-du-29-octobre-2019-relative-a-la-protection-des-donnees-a-caractere-personnel-in-JO29102019.pdf
[The he Electronic Transactions Act No. 2017-007 adopted in 2017](http://jo.gouv.tg/sites/default/files/JO/JOS_07_07_2017-62E%20ANNEE%20N%C2%B021%20QUARTO.pdf)

question: Existence
What is the nature of the framework?
response: A framework exists and has the force of law.

question: 2;Please identify the framework(s) you have assessed (e.g. name of law(s) or regulations)
response:
The Togolese Constitution of October 14, 1992 lays the framework for data protection and privacy and guarantees the "respect for the private life, honor, dignity and image" of every citizen. Article 29 of the constitution states that "the State guarantees the secrecy of correspondence and telecommunications. Every citizen has the right to the secrecy of his correspondence and of his communications and telecommunications". (1). 

On the question of the protection of personal data, recent digital laws are becoming more precise as they are enacted. In order of appearance, the Electronic Communications Act No. 2012-018 (2)(amended by Law No. 2013-003) and the Electronic Transactions Act No. 2017-007 adopted in 2017 was the first law in Togo's history which refers to the concept of "personal data" clearly. (3)

The law dedicated to the protection of personal data in Togo is the Data Protection Act (DPA) n°2019-014 of October 29, 2019 relating the protection of personal data. It regulates the collection, processing, transmission, storage and use of personal data. It applies to individuals, the State, local communities, private and public companies, as well as to automated or non-automated processing of data carried out within the territory of Togo or in any jurisdiction where the Togolese laws apply (4). 

SOURCES:
(1) https://www.constituteproject.org/constitution/Togo_2007.pdf?lang=en 
(2) http://www.artp.tg/Download/Telecommunication/Loi/loi_2013-003_portant_modification_loi_2012-018_du_17_decembre_2012_communications_electorniques.pdf 
(3)http://jo.gouv.tg/sites/default/files/JO/JOS_07_07_2017-62E%20ANNEE%20N%C2%B021%20QUARTO.pdf

(4) https://numerique.gouv.tg/wp-content/uploads/2020/01/Loi-n-2019-014-du-29-octobre-2019-relative-a-la-protection-des-donnees-a-caractere-personnel-in-JO29102019.pdf

question: Elements
Rights and responsibilities:
The framework provides data subjects with rights of choice or consent.
2;Please indicate which section of the framework refers to this issue.
response:
The section 3 of the Data Protection Act (1) defines the basic principles governing the processing of personal data. The Article 14 of the Data Protection Act defines the principle of consent and legitimacy.

Article 3 states that: 

This law does not apply:
1) data processing carried out by a
natural person within the exclusive framework of its activities
personal or household, provided that the data
are not intended for systematic communication
to third parties or to distribution;
2) temporary copies made as part of the technical activities of transmission and provision of access to
a digital network, for automatic storage, intermediary and transient of data and for the sole purpose of
allow other recipients of the best service
possible access to the information transmitted.


The Article 14 states that: 

The processing of personal data is considered legitimate if the data subject gives his
consent. However, this requirement may be waived when the
treatment is necessary:
1) compliance with a legal obligation to which the controller is subject;
2) the performance of a mission of public interest or relevant
the exercise of public authority vested in the controller or the third party to whom the data is
communicated;
3) the performance of a contract to which the data subject
is a party or to the execution of pre-contractual measures
taken at his request;
4) to safeguard the interest or fundamental rights and freedoms of the data subject.


Sources:  
(1) https://numerique.gouv.tg/wp-content/uploads/2020/01/Loi-n-2019-014-du-29-octobre-2019-relative-a-la-protection-des-donnees-a-caractere-personnel-in-JO29102019.pdf

question: The framework provides data subjects with rights to access and correct data about themselves.
2;Please indicate which section of the framework refers to this issue.
response:
Section 2 of the Data Protection Act (1) indicates that data subjects have rights to access and correct data about themselves. According to the article 39,  data subjects proving their identity has the right to request, in writing, regardless of the medium, the person responsible for processing personal data, to provide him with information making it possible to know and contest the treatment of its data.

Sources:  
(1) https://numerique.gouv.tg/wp-content/uploads/2020/01/Loi-n-2019-014-du-29-octobre-2019-relative-a-la-protection-des-donnees-a-caractere-personnel-in-JO29102019.pdf

question: The framework sets out clear responsibilities for data holders.
2;Please indicate which section of the framework refers to this issue.
response:
The chapter III of the Data Protection Act in its articles 79 to Article 93 (1)  sets out clear responsibilities for data holders including fines and legal proceeding for Illicit processing of sensitive data, non-compliance with security measures, fraudulent processing of personal data, non-compliance with the right to object etc.

Sources:  
(1) https://numerique.gouv.tg/wp-content/uploads/2020/01/Loi-n-2019-014-du-29-octobre-2019-relative-a-la-protection-des-donnees-a-caractere-personnel-in-JO29102019.pdf

question: The framework provides rights of redress
2;Please indicate which section of the framework refers to this issue.
response:
The chapter 5 of the Data protection Act (1) provides rights of redress through the the Personal Data Protection Authority. The article the article 55 of the law define how Individuals and communities should have the right of redress against public and private bodies that fail to respect data protection rules in relation to data about them. The right of redress is not yet applicable because the authority in charge is not yet established as required by the law. 

Sources:  
(1) https://numerique.gouv.tg/wp-content/uploads/2020/01/Loi-n-2019-014-du-29-octobre-2019-relative-a-la-protection-des-donnees-a-caractere-personnel-in-JO29102019.pdf

To what extent do relevant laws, regulations, policies, and guidance provide a comprehensive framework for data sharing?
response:
In Togo, The laws do not explicitly mention data sharing. They refer more to making information available to applicants and sharing information in between government agencies, departments etc. And in some cases this data is only available on request or on-site consultation. The only law which made mention of data sharing in the context of the law is the Law 2017-07 of June 22, 2017 relating to electronic transactions (1). 


Sources:

(1) The Law n ° 2017‐07 of June 22, 2017 relating to electronic transactions, http://www.droit-afrique.com/uploads/Togo-Loi-2017-07-transactions-electroniques.pdf
[The Law n ° 2017‐07 of June 22, 2017 relating to electronic transactions,](http://www.droit-afrique.com/uploads/Togo-Loi-2017-07-transactions-electroniques.pdf)

The Law n ° 2017‐07 of June 22, 2017 (1) relating to electronic transactions has made reference to data sharing. This law establishes the general rules governing any electronic transaction, any transaction of any nature whatsoever, taking the form of an electronic message. Also The law on statistics partially mentions the subject but limited only to the publication of its data by state entities. 

Sources:
(1)http://www.droit-afrique.com/uploads/Togo-Loi-2017-07-transactions-electroniques.pdf

question: 0,1;If there are draft laws or regulations not yet in force, but that would provide a more robust framework in future, please provide brief details here.
Elements

The framework covers data sharing within government.
1;Please indicate which section of the framework refers to this issue and explain your 'Partially' response.
response:
In its article 125, the Law n ° 2017‐07 of June 22, 2017 relating to electronic transactions (1) made a reference to data to be collected in the context of administrative and legal proceedings, under the terms of the law. According to the law, when it is required that a supporting document be attached in support of a request or a declaration sent to the administration, the applicant is exempted from providing this document electronically when the administration can obtain it directly to the administration and departments concerned. 
In this case, the provision of the document is replaced by a declaration on the honor of the applicant, who has the possibility of electronically verifying the information
taken into account by the administration. What this means is that, public service can share data of applicants among each other. 

Sources : 

(1) http://www.droit-afrique.com/uploads/Togo-Loi-2017-07-transactions-electroniques.pdf

question: Existence
Are there laws, policies, or regulations requiring collection or publication of this information in any form?
1,2;Please provide brief details.
response:
Law 2016-006 03/30/2016, Freedom of access to information and public documentation  (1). The objective of the law as specified in its first article is to safeguard and to specify the citizen's right to information and public documents. The law applies to information produced or held by a public service as part of its mission and attributions and also applies to conservation of this information by public bodies. The information includes any original or copy of a document whatever its physical characteristics, such as correspondence, facts, opinions, memos, data, statistics, books, drawings, plans, maps, diagrams, photographs, audiovisual recordings, electronic, and any other tangible or intangible document.

question: The framework requires a verification process.
1;Please briefly explain the verification process used, and which parts of collected data the framework requires to be verified and which parts it does not.
response:
The control and verification of the regularity of financial transactions are carried out by control bodies, in particular the Court of Auditors (1). The Court of Auditors gives its opinion on the internal control system and the management control system, on the quality of the accounting procedures and accounts as well as on the annual performance reports. This opinion is accompanied by recommendations on desirable improvements. It is judicial control. It is supplemented by the control of the executive itself, ensured by the financial controllers. The latter report to the Minister in charge of finance and are appointed by him or at his initiative to the authorizing officers. They are responsible for the a priori controls of budgetary operations. They can give opinions on the quality of the management of authorizing officers and on performance. Finally, parliamentary control is ensured by the finance committees, which oversee the proper execution of finance laws during annual management
programs.

Sources: 
(1) https://www.courdescomptes.tg/wp-content/uploads/2021/01/Loi-organique-n%C2%B02014-013-relative-au-Lois-de-Finances.pdf
